metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the close-gate behavior for legacy retirement events in the 114f-only window: either (a) have the no-op also clear the `state['signature']` mirror (so the mirror reflects the blank record, preserving old gate behavior) or (b) explicitly document that retire_attested_pin must be removed from transition.py SIMULTANEOUSLY with 114f shipping (not left to 929e), so no new blank events can be written.
findings.finding: The retirement-as-no-op rule creates a behavioral regression for the close-gate in the 114f-only deployment window (before 929e ships): under the old code a blank/retired SIGNATURE event writes manifest=[], signature='' to state['signature'], so verify_record returns 'unsigned'; under the new no-op rule the mirror retains the prior non-blank attestation, so verify_record returns 'certified' — the opposite. The plan claims 'same gate outcome' but the validity-on-read mitigation is 929e scope, not 114f scope.
findings.location: Edge cases (pinned) — RETIREMENT / BLANK EVENTS section; AC edge-cases line 2
findings.suggested_fix: The simplest fix that preserves old gate behavior: when process_signature sees an empty manifest OR retired=True, treat it as a no-op for `attestations` but still write the blank record to `state['signature']` (the mirror). This preserves the existing close-gate semantics — verify_record sees manifest=[] and returns 'unsigned' — until 929e's validity-on-read replaces the mirror-read entirely.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a testing section (or per-AC test notes) covering: (a) `process_signature` routing — manifest-kind match, mismatch/hint-ignored, blank manifest no-op, retired=True no-op; (b) fold-in — valid legacy record filed under correct kind, blank/unkindable record dropped; (c) `public_state()` — HMAC hex absent from every per-kind attestation record in output; (d) retirement event replay → attestation present but compute_validity returns not-valid after reopen.
findings.finding: The plan introduces multiple new conditional behaviors (manifest[0] routing vs data["kind"] hint, fold-in with blank/unkindable drop, retirement/blank event no-op, HMAC-strip in public_state per-kind) but contains zero mention of test authoring, test file modifications, or test coverage for changed/deleted behaviors.
findings.location: ## Acceptance Criteria / ## Edge cases / ## Acceptance Criteria (edge cases) — no testing section present anywhere

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define `compute_validity` (or its equivalent): specify its location (file path), inputs (`state`, `kind`?), return shape, and the exact boolean rule that uses `last_reopened_at` vs `signed_at` to determine not-valid.
findings.finding: `compute_validity` is referenced as the consumer of `state['last_reopened_at']` for the validity-on-read gate, but this function does not exist anywhere in the codebase and its protocol (inputs, return value, gate logic) is never defined in the plan.
findings.location: ## What — 'so compute_validity can check "not reopened since signed_at" purely from compiled state'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or expand AC2) that verifies `compute_validity` correctly reads `last_reopened_at` and returns not-valid for an attestation signed before the most recent reopen timestamp.
findings.finding: The plan describes `compute_validity` as a consumer of `last_reopened_at` ('so `compute_validity` can check "not reopened since signed_at" purely from compiled state') but no AC verifies this consumer path works correctly with the new field.
findings.location: What section — 'so `compute_validity` can check "not reopened since signed_at" purely from compiled state'
findings.suggested_fix: Expand AC2 to: '`state["last_reopened_at"]` records the most recent closed→open timestamp (null if never reopened); `compute_validity` treats an attestation as not-valid when `signed_at < last_reopened_at`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either add `compute_validity` (or its call-site) to the Scope file list and add an AC that proves a reopened ticket reads NOT-valid through it, OR explicitly defer validity-on-read to a named follow-on slice and remove the present-tense "so compute_validity can check" claim from this plan.
findings.finding: `compute_validity` is cited as the consumer of `state["last_reopened_at"]` ("so compute_validity can check 'not reopened since signed_at' purely from compiled state") but `compute_validity` is absent from the Scope file list and has no AC confirming it is updated in this slice. The non-deferred goal of validity-on-read therefore lacks an end-state-observable proof within this plan.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Scope: the file containing `compute_validity`. Add AC: 'A ticket signed before a closed→open transition returns is_valid=False from compute_validity after the reopen event is processed.' If this is intentionally deferred, rewrite the "Why" sentence as 'this stores the data that will allow a follow-on slice to update compute_validity.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Capture the dual-write mirror pattern (attestations[kind] + state["signature"] compatibility shim, its intended lifetime, and the four named legacy consumers) in an ADR, CLAUDE.md section, or design doc so future slices have a written contract for when the mirror can be retired.
findings.finding: The dual-write mirror pattern (writing both `attestations[kind]` and the legacy `state["signature"]` most-recent mirror) is a novel architectural decision and a deliberate compatibility shim that future slices depend on understanding, but no ADR, CLAUDE.md, or design-doc update is mentioned in the plan.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a short ADR or CLAUDE.md entry covering: (1) why the mirror exists, (2) which consumers it unblocks, (3) the condition for removing it (all four consumers migrated to `attestations`), and (4) the fold-in / snapshot round-trip invariant.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the exact comparison for the `data['kind']` hint validation: specify that 'matches' means `data['kind'] == manifest[0].split(':', 1)[0]` (or the chosen operator) and co-locate this rule with the `process_signature` spec.
findings.finding: The 'data["kind"] hint validation' match rule is referenced but its equality/comparison operator and comparison target are never defined — an implementer cannot determine whether to compare data['kind'] against the pre-colon substring of manifest[0], the full manifest[0] string, or something else.
findings.location: ## What — 'Kind is from the SIGNED manifest[0] (substring before first ":")'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the ## What section: 'Hint validation: `data["kind"]` matches iff `data["kind"] == kind_from_manifest` where `kind_from_manifest = manifest[0].split(":", 1)[0]` (the pre-colon substring). If the hint is present but does not match, route under `kind_from_manifest` anyway (the manifest is authoritative).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the fallback for a data['kind'] hint mismatch: state explicitly whether a mismatching hint causes the event to (A) route under the manifest-derived kind anyway, or (B) become a no-op for routing (and document which field wins — the plan text implies (A) since 'manifest[0] is authoritative', but this must be stated).
findings.finding: The fallback when `data['kind']` hint is present but does NOT match the manifest-derived kind is unspecified — the plan says 'used iff it matches, else ignored' but does not say whether a mismatching hint causes the event to route under the manifest-derived kind or become a no-op.
findings.location: ## What — 'data["kind"] is only a hint, used iff it matches, else ignored'
findings.suggested_fix: Add: 'A mismatching hint is silently discarded; the event still routes under the manifest-derived kind (manifest[0] is always authoritative for routing, and data["kind"] can only confirm, never override).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Consider deferring `state["last_reopened_at"]` + `process_status` changes to the slice that introduces `compute_validity`, or add an explicit AC in THIS ticket that exercises a round-trip assertion on the field (closed→open transition recorded, never-reopened → null) to justify its inclusion here.
findings.finding: `last_reopened_at` is added to the state model in this ticket but has no consumer within this ticket's own ACs — its only stated consumer (`compute_validity`) belongs to a future slice, making it a YAGNI addition that inflates scope without being exercised here.
findings.location: ## What — 'Also add state["last_reopened_at"]' / AC line 'state["last_reopened_at"] records the most recent closed->open timestamp'
findings.suggested_fix: Move the `last_reopened_at` addition to the `compute_validity` slice where it has a real consumer and can be end-to-end tested. If it must stay here, add an AC: 'process_status: first closed→open transition sets last_reopened_at; subsequent reopens update it; a ticket never closed stays null; a snapshot round-trip preserves the value.'

findings.finding: No file_impact declared on a leaf work ticket.
findings.suggested_fix: Record the {path, reason} files this work will touch (e.g. via set_file_impact) so the attestation is scoped to them.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out note: since reduce is a pure fold over the immutable event log, re-running `compact` from the full log regenerates correct compiled state — document this as the recovery path, and confirm no fold-in irreversibility exists beyond the log itself.
findings.finding: The plan explicitly labels this 'the riskiest slice (back-compat + compaction)' but provides no rollback or back-out plan for the fold-in DROP and schema changes; expand-contract covers only the signature mirror, not the irreversibility of dropped blank/unkindable records or a broken schema migration.
findings.location: Why section — 'the riskiest slice (back-compat + compaction), done first'; Edge cases — FOLD-IN KIND drop rule
findings.suggested_fix: Add a Rollback section: 'Because `reduce` is a pure fold over the immutable event log, any incorrect compiled state (including a bad fold-in) is recoverable by re-running compaction from the full log. The schema change is additive (new optional keys); removing `attestations`/`last_reopened_at` from the schema restores old behavior for old tooling.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the exact role of `data["kind"]` as a hint: specify (a) what it must match (manifest[0]-derived kind?), (b) what happens on mismatch (proceed with manifest[0]-derived kind? or drop?), and (c) whether it is ever used when manifest[0] is absent/blank (currently the plan says blank manifest is a no-op, making the hint meaningless in that case).
findings.finding: The phrase 'data["kind"] is only a hint, used iff it matches, else ignored' does not specify what it must match against. The most plausible reading is 'matches the kind derived from manifest[0]', but an alternate reading is 'matches some allowlist/schema'. Worse, if manifest[0] is always authoritative and blank/unkindable manifest is a no-op, it is unclear what execution path ever causes data["kind"] to affect behavior — making 'used iff it matches' effectively dead language. An executing agent cannot implement this without guessing.
findings.location: What section: 'data["kind"] is only a hint, used iff it matches, else ignored'; also AC1: 'routing by authoritative manifest[0] (data["kind"] only as validated hint)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'data["kind"] is only a hint, used iff it matches, else ignored' with: 'data["kind"] is a read-only cross-check — if present, assert it equals the manifest[0]-derived kind; if it differs, log a warning but still route by manifest[0]. data["kind"] is never used as a fallback when manifest is blank/unkindable (those events remain no-ops).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC that verifies `ticket_state.schema.json` is updated to include `attestations` and `last_reopened_at`, and that existing schema-validation tooling (fsck/validate, show) passes against a state carrying both fields.
findings.finding: `ticket_state.schema.json` is listed in Scope as a modified file but no Acceptance Criterion verifies that the schema change is correct, backward-compatible for readers, or round-trips through validation tooling.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to main ACs: '`ticket_state.schema.json` declares `attestations` (object, additionalProperties: per-kind record shape) and `last_reopened_at` (string|null); `fsck`/validate accepts states with and without `attestations` (old snapshots).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either inline the canonical per-kind attestation record shape (field names and types) directly in this plan, or provide a resolvable reference to 114f (e.g., link, file path, or schema excerpt) so an executing agent can proceed without external look-up.
findings.finding: '114f owns the canonical per-record shape' is the sole specification of the attestations record schema. The identifier '114f' is not defined anywhere in this plan, leaving an executing agent unable to determine the exact field set required for attestations[kind] records without consulting an external document.
findings.location: What section: 'Update _present.py public_state() ... 114f owns the canonical per-record shape.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the 'data["kind"] as hint' language in the What section with the Edge Cases no-op rule: if data["kind"] is never a fallback for blank-manifest events, remove the 'used iff it matches' clause from What and restrict the hint description to its actual cross-check-only role.
findings.finding: The What section implies data["kind"] participates in routing ('used iff it matches'), but the Edge Cases section establishes that a blank/unkindable manifest is unconditionally a no-op with no data["kind"] fallback — making the hint logic in What a dead branch. These two sections produce contradictory execution paths for the blank-manifest case.
findings.location: What section vs. Edge Cases section: RETIREMENT / BLANK EVENTS and FOLD-IN KIND paragraphs

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either inline the minimal per-kind record shape (at least the `signature` subkey that must be stripped) into this plan, or make AC4 explicit about which subkey(s) are stripped (e.g., 'the `signature` (HMAC hex) subkey is removed from each per-kind record in `attestations` before public exposure').
findings.finding: The plan delegates the canonical per-record shape to '114f' ('114f owns the canonical per-record shape') — an undefined external slice reference — meaning AC4 ('strips the HMAC hex from EVERY per-kind record') is unverifiable in this slice because the record shape is not defined here.
findings.location: What section — '114f owns the canonical per-record shape'; AC4
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC4: 'Each per-kind record in `attestations` has its `signature` (HMAC hex) subkey removed by `public_state()`; at minimum the same subkeys stripped from the legacy flat `state["signature"]` record are stripped from each kind entry.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate the known and anticipated consumers of `state["attestations"]` (e.g., compute_validity, public_state/show/MCP, any close-gate logic that will migrate off the mirror) alongside the existing `state["signature"]` consumer list, so the full read-side lifecycle is visible in this foundational slice.
findings.finding: Consumers of the new `state["attestations"]` map are not enumerated in this slice, even though downstream slices (e.g., compute_validity, the close-gate path) will read from it; only the four legacy `state["signature"]` consumers are listed.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the opaque '114f' reference with an explicit inline definition of the per-kind record shape (list every key: manifest, algorithm, signature, key_id, head_sha, signed_at, author) in the ## What section so public_state() and process_signature share one authoritative definition.
findings.finding: The reference '114f owns the canonical per-kind record shape' is unresolvable — no file, ticket id, or schema section named '114f' exists in the codebase — leaving the per-kind record shape (what keys each `attestations[kind]` value carries) undefined for `public_state()` implementers.
findings.location: ## What — 'Update _present.py public_state() … 114f owns the canonical per-record shape.'
findings.suggested_fix: Inline the record shape: 'Each attestations[kind] value has keys: {manifest, algorithm, signature, key_id, head_sha, signed_at, author} — identical to the current state["signature"] record. public_state() strips the "signature" subkey from each.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
